tantrums.definition: Sudden outbursts of anger, usually by a child, with crying or screaming.
tantrums.example: Tantrums are common in young children.

observe.definition: To watch carefully and notice things.
observe.example: Observe your child to understand what they are feeling.

premeditated.definition: Planned or thought about before doing it.
premeditated.example: A tantrum is not a premeditated tactic.

communicating.definition: Sharing information, ideas, or feelings with others.
communicating.example: Behaviour is a way of communicating feelings.

restored.definition: Brought back to a normal or calm state.
restored.example: Once calm is restored, talk to the child about better ways.

acceptable.example: Help your child find an acceptable way to express anger.

forbidden.definition: Not allowed; something you are told you cannot have or do.
forbidden.example: The forbidden ice cream was soon forgotten during the tantrum.

caregiver.definition: A person who takes care of a child or someone who needs help.
caregiver.example: The caregiver stayed with the child during the meltdown.

dialogue.definition: A conversation between two or more people.
dialogue.example: Stay in dialogue with a child during a tantrum.

sympathetic.definition: Showing that you understand and care about someone's problems.
sympathetic.example: A sympathetic response helps the child feel less alone.

deliberately.definition: Done on purpose, not by accident.
deliberately.example: From the child's view, you are deliberately misunderstanding them.

extreme.definition: Very great or very strong; much more than normal.
extreme.example: No child should go through extreme distress alone.

comfort.definition: To make someone feel better when they are sad or worried.
comfort.example: Comfort your child even if you cannot give them what they want.

desirable.example: Giving them what they want might not be desirable.

sharks.definition: Large, dangerous fish that live in the ocean.
sharks.example: The child cried because he could not swim with sharks.

perspective.definition: A way of looking at or thinking about something.
perspective.example: Try to see the situation from the child's perspective.

contain.definition: To hold or manage strong feelings so they do not get out of control.
contain.example: A child learns to contain feelings when someone contains them first.

boundary.definition: A rule or limit that tells someone what is not allowed.
boundary.example: Sometimes parents fear putting down a boundary.

scooter.definition: A small vehicle with two wheels that a child stands on and pushes with one foot.
scooter.example: The parent was carrying the scooter all day.

healed.definition: Made well or better after being hurt.
healed.example: No one was ever healed by being made to feel ashamed.

ashamed.definition: Feeling embarrassed or guilty about something.
ashamed.example: Making a child feel ashamed does not help them.

meltdown.definition: A complete loss of control of emotions, like a tantrum.
meltdown.example: You can contain your child during a meltdown.

gestures.definition: Movements of the body, especially hands, that express feelings.
gestures.example: Loving gestures can help a child feel safe.

retaliate.definition: To do something harmful back to someone who hurt you.
retaliate.example: Shouting back at a child is a way to retaliate.

roughly.definition: In a harsh or not gentle way.
roughly.example: Handling your child roughly makes the tantrum worse.

ignoring.definition: Acting as if someone or something is not there.
ignoring.example: Ignoring a tantrum is also a form of retaliation.

pushchair.definition: A small chair on wheels used to push a baby or young child around (stroller).
pushchair.example: Stop pushing the pushchair and talk to the crying child.

sobbing.definition: Crying very hard with big breaths.
sobbing.example: The sobbing child needed a cuddle.

verbalize.definition: To put feelings or thoughts into words.
verbalize.example: I used to verbalize what I thought my child was feeling.

inwardly.definition: Inside yourself, without showing it to others.
inwardly.example: I inwardly marvelled at how far we had come.

marvel.definition: To feel great surprise and wonder about something.
marvel.example: I marvelled when she began to say her feelings out loud.

reflect.definition: To think carefully about something before acting.
reflect.example: Remember to reflect rather than react.

personally.definition: As if it is aimed at you or about you.

triggers.definition: Things that cause a strong reaction or emotion.
triggers.example: Learn your child's triggers for losing control.

ward off.definition: To prevent something bad from happening.
ward off.example: You can ward off a tantrum before it happens.

withdraw.definition: To move away from a group or situation.
withdraw.example: Sometimes a child needs to withdraw from a group for quiet time.

constraint.definition: Something that limits what you can do.
constraint.example: Constraint in a pushchair can be too much for a child.

hunger.example: Have a meal before hunger takes a hold.

permanent.example: No child is in permanent meltdown.

altercation.example: Make a note of the where and when of each altercation.

overstimulation.definition: Too much activity or excitement for someone to handle.

transitions.definition: Changes from one activity or situation to another.
transitions.example: Transitions, like stopping play, can be hard for children.

impatient.definition: Not willing to wait; easily annoyed by delays.
impatient.example: Tantrums are more common when a parent is impatient.

expectation.definition: A belief about what should happen or what someone should do.
expectation.example: Sometimes we put too high an expectation on a child.

impose.example: Don't impose expectations before a child is ready.

frustrate.definition: To make someone feel annoyed or upset because they cannot do what they want.
frustrate.example: Too-high expectations will frustrate both you and your child.

stubborn.definition: Refusing to change your mind or give in.
stubborn.example: Being inflexible sets an example of being stubborn.

escalate.definition: To become worse or more serious.
escalate.example: Strictness can cause the situation to escalate.

forego.definition: To go without something; to give something up.
forego.example: They made the child forego break time.

fidgeting.definition: Making small movements because you cannot stay still.
fidgeting.example: The fidgeting child needed to run around outside.

concentrate.definition: To give all your attention to one thing.
concentrate.example: The child could not concentrate because he needed to move.

excess.definition: More than the normal or needed amount.
excess.example: The child had an excess of physical energy.

extending.example: The school tried extending break time to one hour.

disciplinary.definition: Related to punishing or correcting bad behaviour.
disciplinary.example: There was a drop in disciplinary issues.

initiative.definition: The ability to do things on your own without being told.
initiative.example: Children showed more initiative in solving problems.

creative.definition: Good at thinking of new ideas or making things.
creative.example: The children became more creative at home.

clamping down.definition: Using strict rules to stop something.
clamping down.example: Clamping down on children is rarely the answer.

reasoning.definition: Using facts and logic to make a point.
reasoning.example: Reasoning with a crying child usually does not work.

irritated.example: Parents feel irritated but rarely look at themselves.

co-created.definition: Made together by two or more people.
co-created.example: Any situation between you and your child is co-created.

bearing.definition: An effect or influence on something.
bearing.example: We do have a bearing on how our children behave.

collaboration.example: Think about how to model cooperation and collaboration.
